text: We present Graph of Thoughts (GoT), a novel method that leverages the flexibility of the output of large language models (LLMs) to demonstrate more efficient and effective prompting than state-of-the-art approaches such as Chain-of-Thought (CoT) and Tree of Thoughts (ToT). Our approach is inspired by the idea that LLM outputs can be considered as graphs where individual outputs of the LLM (we call them “LLM thoughts”) are considered as vertices and the edges between vertices describe the relationship between the vertices. Thus, in GoT, an LLM thought can be combined with other LLM thoughts to produce the final output of the LLM, or the sum of a subset of LLM thoughts in a graph can be taken as the output of the LLM, or the output of an LLM thought can be modified based on a feedback loop. We show that our proposed approach has a significant advantage over state-of-the-art approaches in terms of output quality and computational cost, e.g., the output quality for the sorting task is 62% higher than that of ToT and computational cost is more than 31% lower. We also show that our proposed approach can be extended by introducing new thought transformations. Our work therefore suggests that our proposed approach can be used as a pioneering approach for new and innovative methods for leveraging LLMs. Our proposed approach brings the LLM closer to the way that humans think and/or with the way that the human brain works, e.g., using recurrence in the brain to form complex networks of ideas.
